behaviors.description: The review input is scoped to the lines that changed, not the full content of each modified file.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: the agent reads the entire file from disk instead of limiting analysis to the diff. A Unit test with mocks could pass even if the agent naively reads the filesystem. An Integration test—mocking the GitHub diff endpoint to return a specific set of changed lines, creating source code where analyzing the full file produces different findings—would catch this. We don't need real GitHub (System) because the failure is purely in the agent's logic of respecting the diff boundary, not in API edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Provide the agent with a mock GitHub client returning a diff specifying lines 10-20 changed. Create source code with a hidden defect outside those lines (e.g., line 50). Verify the agent generates findings only for lines 10-20 and does not surface the line-50 defect. Track what input the analysis engine received and confirm it was limited to the changed line numbers.

behaviors.description: Inline comments are attached to the correct positions in the diff view on the pull request.
behaviors.justification: GitHub's diff format is subtle: hunks, context lines, renames, deletions, and line numbering all interact. An Integration test with a mock GitHub API might pass if the mock naively accepts any line number. The real failure boundary is 'does the agent correctly map internal line numbers to GitHub's diff-position semantics?' This requires either a real PR with real GitHub diff parsing or a test against an actual (or near-actual) PR created in a test environment. Edge cases (file additions without prior history, line deletions) only surface at this level.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test pull request in a real or staging GitHub environment with intentional changes at various positions (beginning, middle, end of file; multiple files). Run the agent and verify each inline comment appears at the correct line in GitHub's diff view. Validate by checking the comment's position matches the code location it references. Include edge cases: added files, deleted lines, renamed files.

behaviors.description: The agent identifies at least one real defect in a pull request that contains a known introduced bug.
behaviors.justification: This behavior explicitly requires the agent to identify a 'real defect' in a PR with a 'known introduced bug'. If the agent is LLM-powered, this depends entirely on the LLM's non-deterministic capability to spot the defect. Mocking the LLM (Integration) defeats the purpose—a mock always returns 'defect found'. The real failure is: the LLM misses the bug or doesn't classify it as problematic. Only running the real agent against a real buggy PR (Agentic) captures this. The test may pass sometimes and fail others due to LLM variance—that's expected and valuable.
behaviors.test_description: Create or select a pull request with a deliberately introduced bug (e.g., missing null check, off-by-one error in loop, unhandled exception path). Run the real agent (with actual LLM inference) against it. Verify the agent identified at least one legitimate defect in the PR, either the seeded bug or another real issue. Repeat 3+ times to account for LLM non-determinism and confirm the agent catches the defect consistently.

behaviors.description: Every finding listed in the top-level summary corresponds to an inline comment that was also posted — no findings appear in the summary without a matching inline comment.
behaviors.justification: This is an orchestration invariant: the agent must wire together three steps (analysis → inline comment posting → summary generation) such that the summary only references findings that have already been posted inline. A mock GitHub client that tracks all method calls is sufficient to verify this; we don't need real GitHub (System) because the failure is purely in the agent's internal logic, not in API edge cases. A Unit test can't verify the invariant across multiple workflow stages.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the GitHub API client to record all calls to post_inline_comment() and post_summary_comment(). Run the agent on a test PR (with mocked analysis returning 5 findings). Collect all findings from logged inline comments and parse the summary comment text. Verify: every finding mentioned in the summary has a corresponding inline comment posted with matching content/location. Fail if summary contains a finding without a corresponding inline comment.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — reading the diff, posting inline comments, posting the summary comment — completes correctly for a real pull request without manual intervention.
behaviors.justification: The behavior specifies 'a real pull request without manual intervention', indicating an end-to-end test with real (or near-real) GitHub infrastructure. The failure modes—API rate limiting, auth errors, malformed responses from actual GitHub, network timeouts on large repos, or edge cases in real PR state—only surface at System level. Integration tests with mocks mask these. The phrase 'without manual intervention' requires the agent to autonomously handle GitHub's real API contract, error conditions, and response formats.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test pull request on a real or staging GitHub repository with 3–5 code changes and realistic issues. Run the agent end-to-end in a controlled environment (e.g., staging GitHub org). Verify: (1) agent completes without errors or user prompts, (2) inline comments are posted to the correct locations, (3) a summary comment is posted, (4) no API rate limits or auth failures occur. Monitor logs for unexpected exceptions or API call failures. Validate the workflow succeeds on PRs of varying sizes and complexity.
